title: Gestalt Psychology — The Foundation of Visual Perception
content: Gestalt psychology (German for 'unified whole') explains how humans perceive visual information. The brain doesn't process elements one by one — it seeks patterns, groups, and wholes before individual parts. The core insight: the whole is different from the sum of its parts. For UI/UX design, this means users interpret your layout holistically before reading a single word. Get the gestalt wrong and even perfect copy won't save you.

title: Gestalt: Law of Proximity
content: Elements close to each other are perceived as belonging together. This is the most powerful grouping principle in UI. Use it to: group form fields with their labels, cluster related navigation items, separate unrelated content blocks with spacing rather than lines. The classic mistake: equal spacing between all elements — users can't tell what belongs to what. Tight inner spacing + generous outer spacing = clear groups.
example: A label 4px from its input vs 16px from the next label — the label is clearly 'attached' to its field.

title: Gestalt: Law of Similarity
content: Elements that share visual properties (colour, shape, size, texture) are perceived as related. In UI: all primary buttons should share one style, all destructive actions another. Inconsistent styling breaks similarity and creates cognitive noise. Use similarity to build visual hierarchy: same-weight items feel like peers, different weights signal different roles. Don't make clickable and non-clickable elements look the same.
example: Blue = interactive, grey = disabled, red = destructive — users learn this map instantly.

title: Gestalt: Law of Closure
content: The brain fills in missing information to complete familiar shapes. Users perceive complete forms even when parts are missing. Practical applications: icon design (a partially drawn circle is still a circle), progress indicators (incomplete ring = progress), card edges that bleed off-screen (users infer there's more content). Also explains why broken or incomplete UI elements feel 'wrong' — the brain is working too hard to fill gaps.
example: A circle with a gap at the top is still read as a circle — not an arc.

title: Gestalt: Law of Continuity
content: The eye follows smooth, continuous paths over abrupt changes. Alignment creates invisible lines that guide attention. In UI: grid alignment isn't just aesthetics — it creates paths the eye travels. Left-aligned text creates a strong left edge the eye anchors to. Misaligned elements break the flow and feel 'off' even when users can't articulate why. Use continuity to guide users through a flow — their eye should travel naturally from entry point to CTA.
example: A horizontal row of icons creates a line the eye follows left to right — place your most important action at the end.

title: Gestalt: Law of Figure/Ground
content: The brain separates visual scenes into a subject (figure) and a background (ground). In UI this is fundamental: interactive elements must feel like 'figures' against their background. Problems occur when: modals don't sufficiently dim the background, tooltips blend into content, empty states feel like the interface is broken. Contrast, shadow, and blur are the tools for establishing clear figure/ground separation. Dark mode challenges this — backgrounds and surfaces need even more careful layering.
example: A modal with a semi-transparent overlay — the modal is figure, the page behind is ground.

title: Gestalt: Law of Symmetry
content: Symmetrical compositions feel stable, complete, and trustworthy. Asymmetrical compositions feel dynamic, modern, and directional. Both are valid but serve different purposes. Symmetry: banking, healthcare, legal — signals stability. Asymmetry: creative, startup, fashion — signals energy. In UI layout, pure symmetry can feel static; use near-symmetry (balanced but not identical) for interfaces that need to feel both trustworthy and alive. The golden ratio and rule of thirds are tools for structured asymmetry.
example: Stripe's homepage uses asymmetric layouts with anchored left content — energetic but grounded.

title: Gestalt: Law of Common Fate
content: Elements moving in the same direction are perceived as a group. This is the animation dimension of gestalt. In UI: items that animate together are understood as related. A list of items that slides in together = a group. Staggered animations help users understand hierarchy. Chaos animation (random directions, timing) destroys grouping. Use common fate to: reinforce relationships during transitions, indicate a collection loading, show that a set of items is being processed together.
example: All cards in a grid fading in with a slight stagger communicates 'these are the same type of thing'.

title: Gestalt: Law of Prägnanz (Good Form)
content: The brain prefers the simplest, most stable interpretation of any visual. This is why interfaces with simpler shapes, fewer elements, and cleaner compositions feel more professional and trustworthy. Complexity isn't just harder to use — it feels wrong perceptually. Every unnecessary element forces the brain to work harder. Prägnanz is the perceptual basis for minimalism: not aesthetics, but cognitive efficiency. Remove elements until what's left is the simplest possible form that still communicates.

title: Gestalt Applied to Typography
content: Typography obeys all gestalt laws. Proximity: tight line-height groups lines into a paragraph. Similarity: consistent heading sizes create a type hierarchy users can scan. Closure: drop caps and ligatures exploit the brain's gap-filling. Continuity: justified text creates strong left and right margins the eye follows. Figure/ground: text must have sufficient contrast to read as 'figure' against the page. The most common gestalt failure in type: too-similar sizing between heading levels — the brain can't group the content into sections.
example: H1 at 32px, H2 at 28px — too similar. H1 at 40px, H2 at 24px — clear gestalt jump.

title: Preattentive Attributes — What Users See Before They Think
content: Preattentive attributes are visual properties processed by the brain in under 250ms, before conscious attention. They are: colour, size, shape, orientation, motion, enclosure, intensity, position. Using a single preattentive attribute for the most critical information means users find it instantly without scanning. The classic error: using two attributes simultaneously for different things (e.g. colour for status AND size for importance) — users can't process both at once. Use one preattentive attribute per critical signal.

title: F-Pattern and Z-Pattern — How Users Scan Interfaces
content: Eye-tracking research reveals two dominant scanning patterns. F-Pattern (content-heavy pages): users read the first line fully, then scan down the left edge with shorter horizontal sweeps — forming an F shape. Implication: put critical content in the top-left, don't bury important info in the right column. Z-Pattern (sparse pages): eyes travel top-left → top-right → diagonal → bottom-left → bottom-right. Implication: for landing pages and dashboards, place key elements at the Z anchor points. Neither pattern is fixed — strong visual hierarchy can override them.

title: Heuristic Evaluation — Expert UI Review Against Nielsen's 10 Laws
content: A heuristic evaluation is a structured expert review of an interface against Nielsen's 10 usability heuristics without recruiting users. Process: 3–5 evaluators review independently (1–2 hours each), document violations per heuristic, then consolidate and prioritise findings as a group. Best done before usability testing to identify obvious problems first. Key heuristics: visibility of system status, match between system and real world, user control and freedom, consistency and standards, error prevention, recognition over recall, flexibility, aesthetic and minimalist design, help users recognise and recover from errors, help and documentation. Heuristic violations don't always require fixing — context matters.

title: Running a Heuristic Evaluation in Practice
content: Step 1 — Prepare: define scope (one task, one device, one user group), train evaluators on the 10 heuristics, set up documentation (spreadsheet, Miro board, or NNG's free workbook PDF). Step 2 — Evaluate independently: each evaluator walks through the task alone, noting violations and the heuristic broken. Evaluators must not share findings until all are done — independence is the whole point. Step 3 — Consolidate: affinity-map findings, discuss severity (1–4 scale), identify what to test with users next. Time budget: 1–2 hours per evaluator. Ideal team size: 3–5 people. Solo evaluations miss up to 50% of problems.
source: Nielsen Norman Group, Kate Moran & Kelley Gordon, 2023

title: Severity Ratings in Heuristic Evaluations
content: Not all heuristic violations are equal. Use a 1–4 severity scale: 1 = cosmetic problem (fix if time allows), 2 = minor usability problem (low priority), 3 = major usability problem (important to fix), 4 = usability catastrophe (imperative to fix before launch). Rate severity by: frequency (how often does it occur?), impact (how severely does it affect the user?), persistence (does it happen once or repeatedly?). Severity ratings help product teams prioritise fixes in backlogs and make the business case for UX work.

title: Colour Perception and Psychology in UI
content: Colour perception is both physiological (how the eye works) and cultural (what meanings we've learned). Physiological truths: warm colours (red, orange) advance visually — they feel closer. Cool colours (blue, green) recede. High-contrast colours attract preattentive attention. Cultural associations vary significantly: white = purity in Western design, mourning in some Asian contexts. Blue = trust across most Western markets (banking, tech). Red = danger/stop OR luck/prosperity depending on culture. Design systems must acknowledge both layers. Never rely solely on colour to convey meaning — always pair with shape or text for accessibility.
source: Itten, Josef — The Art of Color
